frameworks.name: slow drifts in decision criterion correction (Lak'20 / Mendonça'20)
frameworks.one_line: Existing convenient correction that subtracts slow drifts from decision criterion

frameworks.name: trial-history bias / systematic updating of decision variables
frameworks.one_line: Trial-by-trial systematic updates of decision variables reveal heuristic and learning strategies

frameworks.name: random drifts in decision variables
frameworks.one_line: Non-systematic drifts that can mimic systematic updating signatures

frameworks.name: model-based inference of decision dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Proposed approach to disambiguate systematic updates from random drifts

core_question: Can the proposed correction for slow drifts in decision criterion robustly recover systematic trial-history updating strategies?
key_assumptions: Trial-history biases reflect systematic updates of decision variables
Random drifts in decision variables can mimic systematic update signatures
Correct inference of updating strategy requires accounting for slow drifts
manipulations: Applied the proposed correction across a variety of assumed updating scenarios
Compared correction performance on real and synthetic datasets
Evaluated against a model-based disambiguation approach
key_findings: Correction fails for a wide range of commonly assumed systematic updating strategies
Correction distorts inference away from veridical strategies toward a narrow subset
Proposed model-based approach successfully disambiguates systematic updates from random drifts on real and synthetic data
interpretations: The slow-drift correction is unreliable as a general tool for inferring trial-history updating strategies
A model-based approach is needed to robustly separate random drifts from systematic decision-variable updates
